text: Title: An open letter to Prof J A Heyns (1928–1994) : a reappraisal of his period as senior lecturer at the Faculty of Theology, Stellenbosch University, 1966–1970 This article, written as a letter to the deceased Prof J A Heyns (1928–1994), offers a reappraisal of the relatively short period that he served as senior lecturer in the Faculty of Theology at Stellenbosch University from 1966 to 1970, a period in which he focused primarily on apologetics and ethics in line with his instruction. The author investigates Heyns’s intellectual roots by way of a sixfold mirror in order to situate this period within the context of intellectual, ideological, orthodox and moral developments of the time. This development is situated against the backdrop of a number of contested elements, such as orthodoxy versus modernity, the influence of the philosopher Henk Stoker and the theologian Herman Bavinck, and the moral conflict created by the implementation of apartheid on the one hand and the resistance against it on the other. The “Reflections in a sixfold mirror” is used to illuminate Heyns’s intellectual roots, but by way of the sixfold mirror it also becomes clear that the author is not so much concerned with evaluating the period in Heyns’s academic career, but rather with reflecting on the universal character of an academic career. Youths’ intellectual courage is characterised by a boldness and rigour that is contingent upon risk. Over time, this boldness and rigour is gradually replaced by the wisdom of experience and the rigidity of custom. In this regard, the article also forms part of the growing number of studies in which Heyns’s theological and historical legacy is re-examined.
